facts.label: THE DENIM JERSEY
facts.story: For the first World Cup ever hosted on American soil, Adidas dressed the U.S. away kit in a faux-denim print with oversized, distorted white stars — an homage to blue jeans that players reportedly laughed about to keep from crying. Design chief Peter Moore's reasoning: 'If people are angry, then at least they're paying attention.' Ridiculed in 1994, the shirt has since become a cult classic — Adidas reissued a retro version decades later, stars, wash, and all.

facts.label: THE SASH THAT BEAT ENGLAND
facts.story: On June 29, 1950, a scratch U.S. side — a dishwasher, two mailmen, and a teacher among them — wore plain white shirts with a single red diagonal sash and navy collar into Belo Horizonte. Haitian-born forward Joe Gaetjens headed in the only goal, upsetting England 1–0 in what's still called one of sport's biggest shocks. Nike revived that red sash for the 2010 World Cup kit, six decades later, as a direct tribute to the jersey that started it.

1930.headline: Patenaude's Historic Hat-Trick
1930.story: Bert Patenaude scored the first hat-trick in World Cup history as the U.S. routed Paraguay 3–0, backing up an opening win over Belgium. Argentina ended the run 6–1 in the semi-final in Montevideo, but with no third-place match played, the U.S. was later credited with third — still the program's best World Cup finish.

annotations.label: THE FIRST HAT-TRICK
annotations.story: Bert Patenaude scored all three goals in a 3–0 win over Paraguay, but FIFA didn't officially recognize it as the first hat-trick in World Cup history until 2006 — seventy-six years later, after re-examining old match reports and resolving a long dispute with an Argentine forward over the record.

annotations.label: THE SHIELD CREST
annotations.story: The 1930 jersey carried no sponsor and no standardized numbering — just a plain white cotton pullover with a small shield crest stitched over the heart, vertical red-and-white stripes beneath a cluster of stars, the same shirt the team wore into the 6–1 semifinal mauling by Argentina in Montevideo.

annotations.label: TRAINER'S CHLOROFORM COLLAPSE
annotations.story: Down 1–0 to Argentina in the semifinal, trainer Jack Coll sprinted onto the field after a brutal foul broke Ralph Tracy's leg — then collapsed himself when a vial of chloroform shattered inside his medical bag, knocking him out cold and forcing teammates to carry two injured men off at once.

1930.homeColors: white shirt, blue shorts, white socks

1934.headline: One Match, Seven Conceded
1934.story: A single-elimination format gave the U.S. no room for error, and Italy — the eventual champions — punished them 7–1 in Rome. Aldo Donelli scored the lone American goal. It remains the heaviest defeat in U.S. World Cup history, a rude return after the expanded 1934 qualifying format.

annotations.label: DONELLI'S LONE STRIKE
annotations.story: Trailing 6–0 to a rampant Italy side at Stadio Nazionale, Aldo Donelli poked in a consolation goal in the 57th minute — the only American touch that mattered in a rout the hosts had already decided before halftime, weeks before Italy lifted the trophy as world champions.

annotations.label: SAME SHIRT, NEW COLOR
annotations.story: Nobody redesigned a thing for Rome — the U.S. wore the identical long-sleeved cut, collar, and shield crest from 1930, simply recolored from white to blue. For a federation with almost no budget and a squad making its second trip of the decade, it was a money-saving choice dressed up as tradition.

annotations.label: FOUR GOALS TO QUALIFY
annotations.story: Ten days before Italy routed them, Aldo Donelli scored all four goals in a win-or-go-home playoff against Mexico in Rome just to reach the tournament, reportedly with Benito Mussolini watching from the stands. It remains the only World Cup the U.S. has ever entered through a single knockout qualifier.

1934.homeColors: white shirt, blue shorts, white socks

1950.headline: Gaetjens Shocks England
1950.story: Joe Gaetjens, a Haitian-born dishwasher playing for pocket money, headed in the only goal as a patchwork American team beat England 1–0 in Belo Horizonte — still considered one of the greatest upsets in sports history. Losses to Spain and Chile followed, but the England result alone made the tournament immortal.

annotations.label: THE SCORELINE NOBODY BELIEVED
annotations.story: When word of the 1–0 shock reached European wire desks, several newspaper editors assumed a transmission error and printed the score as 10–1 to England instead — a number that made far more sense to anyone who hadn't just watched Joe Gaetjens head the ball past the co-favorites in Belo Horizonte.

annotations.label: NO FIXED NUMBERS
annotations.story: 1950 was the first World Cup where players wore numbers on their backs, but nothing was fixed for the tournament — goalkeeper Frank Borghi kept No. 1 through the England upset, while outfield teammates swapped numbers freely between matches, leaving no consistent roster for scorekeepers trying to track who actually played.

annotations.label: THE HEARSE-DRIVING KEEPER
annotations.story: Borghi drove a hearse for his uncle's funeral home in St. Louis before the tournament and went straight back to it afterward. So few Americans cared about a soccer upset in Brazil that he and his teammates stayed essentially anonymous at home for decades, until historians and filmmakers revived the story.

1950.homeColors: white shirt with red diagonal sash, navy blue collar, white shorts, red socks

1990.headline: Caligiuri's Goal, Rough Return
1990.story: Paul Caligiuri's long-range strike against Trinidad and Tobago had gotten the U.S. here, but Italy's hosts proved unforgiving: Czechoslovakia routed them 5–1 in the opener, and losses to Italy and Austria closed a tough return to the World Cup after a 40-year absence, Bruce Murray's goal the lone bright spot.

annotations.label: FIRST AMERICAN RED CARD
annotations.story: One day after turning 21, Eric Wynalda became the first American sent off at a World Cup — shoved by Czechoslovakia's Tomas Skuhravy at a corner kick in the 52nd minute, he swung back and the referee showed red. The Americans finished the 5–1 rout a man down and four decades rusty.

annotations.label: THE BLANK ADIDAS TEMPLATE
annotations.story: Adidas dressed the returning Americans in barely more than a house template — plain white shirt, a few red-and-blue slashes, the crest dropped dead center — because after a 40-year absence there was no established national identity to design around. It photographed more like a training top than a World Cup jersey.

annotations.label: A ROSTER OF PART-TIMERS
annotations.story: The youngest squad in the tournament, averaging 24 years old, was pieced together from the American Soccer League, the Major Indoor Soccer League, and college programs rather than any real professional pipeline — because none existed yet. Only Paul Caligiuri's long-range qualifying goal against Trinidad had gotten this patchwork group to Italy at all.

1990.homeColors: white shirt with red and blue accents, navy blue shorts, white socks (Adidas)

1994.headline: Home Soil, Round of 16
1994.story: Eric Wynalda's free kick salvaged a draw with Switzerland, and an Andrés Escobar own goal beat Colombia to send the hosts through for the first time since 1950. Brazil, the eventual champions, needed a second-half goal to end the run 1–0 in Stanford — a heartbreaking exit on home turf.

annotations.label: EQUALIZER UNDER THE DOME
annotations.story: Eric Wynalda's free kick that tied Switzerland 1–1 came inside the Pontiac Silverdome, the first World Cup match ever staged indoors — real grass grown specially by Michigan State University under a roof with no air conditioning, so humid that Switzerland's own coach later admitted his team hadn't prepared for the heat.

annotations.label: THIRTEEN STRIPES, ONE FLAG
annotations.story: Adidas's white home shirt wasn't random decoration — the red lines rippling across the chest were meant to mirror a flag caught mid-flutter, thirteen stripes for the original colonies, worn beneath a crest that had shrunk compared to earlier decades as national kit design leaned decorative in the U.S. for the first time.

annotations.label: THE OWN GOAL'S AFTERMATH
annotations.story: The 2–1 win over Colombia that kept the Americans alive included an own goal by defender Andrés Escobar, who flew home to Medellín afterward and was shot dead outside a nightclub ten days later — a killing investigators tied to gambling losses riding on that match. The victory still carries that shadow.

1994.homeColors: white shirt with red and blue wave stripes, black shorts, white socks (Adidas)
1994.awayColors: denim-print blue shirt with white stars, red trim, white shorts (Adidas)

1998.headline: Winless in France
1998.story: A last-place finish in a stacked field: Germany, Iran, and FR Yugoslavia all beat the U.S., including a politically charged loss to Iran that dominated headlines more than the football itself. Brian McBride's consolation goal against Iran was the only bright spot in a tournament the team wanted to forget.

annotations.label: ESTILI'S HEADER, MCBRIDE'S ANSWER
annotations.story: Iran took the lead in the 40th minute on Hamid Estili's header, and Brian McBride's headed reply in the 87th minute stood as the only American goal of the entire tournament — arriving too late in a 2–1 loss that ended any hope of escaping the group with zero wins from France.

annotations.label: NIKE'S QUIET DEBUT
annotations.story: Nike's first U.S. World Cup jersey dropped the layered Americana of 1994 entirely for a plain white shirt with one navy stripe and red piping — a switch from Adidas so understated that few fans even noticed. It was a template shirt built for a team nobody expected to make noise anyway.

annotations.label: ROSES BEFORE KICKOFF
annotations.story: Before kickoff in Lyon, under orders that neither side actually walk toward the other first, Iranian players crossed the field anyway and handed the Americans white roses while officials arranged both squads for a joint photograph — a peace gesture staged around a match so politically loaded that FIFA assigned extra stadium security.

1998.homeColors: white shirt, navy blue shorts, red socks (Nike)

2002.headline: Golden Generation's Quarter-Final
2002.story: John O'Brien and Brian McBride starred as the U.S. shocked Portugal 3–2, then eliminated rivals Mexico 2–0 in the Round of 16 behind McBride and Landon Donovan. Germany ended the surprise run 1–0 in the quarter-final, denying the Americans a semi-final berth by the narrowest of margins.

annotations.label: THREE GOALS, ONE UPSET
annotations.story: Eight minutes in, John O'Brien scored the first U.S. World Cup goal against a European power in decades, and by the 36th minute it was 3–0 over Portugal, ranked fifth in the world. Portugal clawed back two late goals, but the Americans held on to open the tournament with a genuine shock.

annotations.label: BUILT FOR BLACK-AND-WHITE TV
annotations.story: Nike engineered the 2002 kits around Coolmotion venting panels to fight Korea-Japan humidity, but designers also solved an older problem — enough households worldwide still watched on black-and-white sets that every kit matchup had to read clearly without color, forcing bold, high-contrast blocking even on the tournament's brightest jerseys.

annotations.label: THE FIRST POST-9/11 CUP
annotations.story: This was the first World Cup the Americans played after September 11, and the run to the quarterfinals landed as unmistakably patriotic — ESPN's pre-dawn broadcasts from Korea drew crowds into bars before sunrise, and beating rival Mexico 2–0 in the Round of 16 became as much a morale story as a soccer one.

2002.homeColors: white shirt, navy blue shorts, white socks (Nike)

2006.headline: Group of Death, No Wins
2006.story: Drawn into a brutal group with Italy, Czech Republic, and Ghana, the U.S. lost the opener 3–0, salvaged a draw with eventual champions Italy on a Cristian Zaccardo own goal, then fell to Ghana 2–1 in the finale — a group-stage exit that undid the promise of 2002.

annotations.label: NINE MEN HOLD ITALY
annotations.story: Pablo Mastroeni and Eddie Pope were both sent off, leaving the Americans a man light for more than 45 minutes against Italy, who themselves played most of the second half down to ten after Daniele De Rossi elbowed Brian McBride bloody. Down to nine men, the U.S. still salvaged a 1–1 draw.

annotations.label: THE 1950 STRIPE RETURNS
annotations.story: Nike's home shirt quietly revived the diagonal stripe from the 1950 sash — thinner this time, dark red and navy running beneath the crest rather than across the whole chest — while the navy away shirt paired an oversized white collar with a thick horizontal band broken by the player's own number.

annotations.label: THE RANKING THAT LIED
annotations.story: The Americans arrived in Germany having been ranked fourth in the world that spring, a number almost nobody inside the sport actually believed. It evaporated fast — a 3–0 opening loss to the Czechs, then a group-stage exit against Ghana, dropping the team to 31st by year's end, the sharpest ranking collapse in program history.

2006.homeColors: white shirt, navy blue shorts, white socks (Nike)

2010.headline: Donovan's Stoppage-Time Miracle
2010.story: Landon Donovan's stoppage-time winner against Algeria — arguably the most celebrated goal in U.S. soccer history — completed a group-stage comeback after draws with England and Slovenia. Ghana ended the run in extra time in the Round of 16, but Donovan's goal alone made 2010 unforgettable.

annotations.label: HOWARD'S FIFTY-YARD OUTLET
annotations.story: The winning sequence started in Tim Howard's hands — he caught a header, then launched a throw nearly fifty yards upfield to a sprinting Landon Donovan before the broadcast cameras even found it. Donovan fed Altidore, Altidore squared it, Dempsey's shot was saved, and Donovan buried the rebound to beat Algeria 1–0.

annotations.label: EIGHT BOTTLES PER JERSEY
annotations.story: Nike built every one of its 2010 national team jerseys, including the U.S. kit, from recycled polyester — roughly eight plastic bottles melted down and rewoven per shirt, much of it sourced from landfill sites in Japan and Taiwan. It was the first American World Cup jersey made from literal garbage.

annotations.label: GROUP WINNERS SINCE 1930
annotations.story: Beating Algeria didn't just cap a comeback through a draw-heavy group — it made the Americans group winners for the first time since the inaugural 1930 tournament, eighty years earlier, setting up a more favorable Round of 16 draw that still ended in extra-time heartbreak against Ghana.

2010.homeColors: white shirt with red diagonal sash (1950 tribute), navy blue shorts (Nike)

2014.headline: The Group of Death, Survived
2014.story: Clint Dempsey scored eight seconds into the opener against Ghana, and a late John Brooks header sealed the win. A Portugal stoppage-time equalizer and a loss to Germany followed, but the U.S. escaped the 'Group of Death' anyway. Belgium ended the run in extra time in the Round of 16.
2014.homeColors: white shirt, navy blue collar and trim (Nike)
2014.awayColors: 'Bomb Pop' — blue top, red bottom, white stripe divider (Nike)

2022.headline: Pulisic's Costly Winner
2022.story: Christian Pulisic's goal against Iran — scored on a play where he suffered a pelvic injury crashing into the goalkeeper — sent the U.S. through as runners-up in a tight group with England and Wales. The Netherlands ended the run 3–1 in the Round of 16, Haji Wright's goal the lone U.S. reply.
2022.homeColors: white shirt with thin red and blue trim, navy blue shorts (Nike)
